Write a Java 8 method to find the sum of all elements in a List of integers.
answer: Multiple approaches exist: reduce() with identity 0 and BinaryOperator Integer::sum, mapToInt() for primitive stream avoiding boxing overhead, or Collectors.summingInt(). The primitive stream approach (mapToInt().sum()) is most efficient because it avoids boxing Integer to int on each element. For large lists, parallelStream() can speed up computation on multi-core machines, though boxing overhead makes this worthwhile only for very large lists.

question: Your Java service is slow but CPU usage is low — what do you investigate first?
answer: Low CPU with slow response is a classic sign of I/O blocking or thread contention. Take a thread dump using jstack and look for threads in WAITING or BLOCKED state. Common culprits: (1) Database — service waiting on slow SQL; check HikariCP pool metrics, if active connections equal max you have pool exhaustion. (2) External HTTP calls — downstream service slow; check timeouts on RestTemplate/WebClient. (3) Lock contention — threads waiting on synchronized blocks; thread dump shows which thread holds the lock. (4) Disk I/O — excessive logging blocking threads. (5) GC pauses — frequent pauses add latency even without high CPU. Approach: thread dump first, then connection pool metrics, then APM traces to find the exact bottleneck. Use Spring Boot Actuator /actuator/metrics/hikaricp.connections.active and /actuator/threaddump.

question: A thread pool is configured correctly, yet tasks are getting delayed. Why?
answer: Even a correctly-sized thread pool delays tasks for non-obvious reasons. (1) Queue saturation: a bounded queue fills when all threads are busy; tasks wait before a thread picks them up. (2) Task duration mismatch: I/O-bound work in a CPU-sized pool means threads spend most time blocked, starving others. For I/O-bound, pool size should be cores / (1 - blocking coefficient). (3) Thread starvation deadlock: tasks submit child tasks to the same pool and wait for results — parent holds threads, children cannot run. (4) CallerRunsPolicy: the submitting thread runs the task, blocking it from submitting more. (5) Interference from scheduled tasks sharing the same pool. Monitor queue depth via ThreadPoolExecutor.getQueue().size() and active count.

question: Your app throws OutOfMemoryError even though heap looks sufficient. How?
answer: OOM scenarios exist outside heap: (1) Metaspace exhaustion — class metadata lives off-heap. Dynamic class generation (CGLIB proxies, Groovy scripts, Reflection) fills Metaspace. Error: OutOfMemoryError: Metaspace. Fix: -XX:MaxMetaspaceSize=256m. (2) Direct/Native memory — NIO ByteBuffer.allocateDirect() uses native memory. Netty and Kafka clients are heavy users. Fix: -XX:MaxDirectMemorySize. (3) GC overhead limit exceeded — heap has free space but GC spends >98% time collecting <2% — thrashing signal. (4) Thread stack memory — thousands of threads each consume 512KB–1MB stack. (5) Heap fragmentation — free space exists but no contiguous block for a large array. Always correlate OOM message type with native memory, Metaspace, and GC logs.

question: Production logs show no errors, but users face random failures. What could be wrong?
answer: Silent failures with no logged errors: (1) Swallowed exceptions — catch blocks logging at DEBUG or silently returning null/empty. Search for catch(Exception e) {} patterns. (2) CompletableFuture exceptions silently dropped unless .exceptionally() or .handle() is attached. A lost exception means the future never completes or returns null. (3) Kafka consumer failure with offset committed anyway — message is lost. (4) @Transactional rollback swallowing the actual cause in a higher-level try-catch. (5) Timeout-based silent failure — HTTP call times out, stale fallback returned without alerting. (6) Race conditions producing wrong data without exceptions. Fix: add .exceptionally() to all futures, set up DLQs for Kafka, use MDC correlation IDs to trace user-specific flows.

question: Multiple threads update shared data and results are inconsistent — how do you fix it?
answer: Inconsistent results from concurrent updates indicate a race condition. Fix depends on access pattern: (1) Atomic primitives — AtomicInteger, AtomicLong, AtomicReference use CAS hardware instructions, no locks needed, for single-variable updates. (2) Synchronized methods/blocks — coarse-grained but correct; minimize scope to reduce contention. (3) ReentrantLock — more flexible, supports timeout and interruptible waits. (4) ConcurrentHashMap — computeIfAbsent and merge are atomic per-key. (5) Immutable objects + copy-on-write — create new objects instead of mutating. (6) Volatile — only for single-variable visibility, NOT sufficient for read-modify-write like count++. (7) Reduce shared state — use local variables, ThreadLocal, or actor model. Identify whether the issue is visibility, atomicity, or ordering.

question: A Java app works fine for hours, then suddenly slows down. What's your approach?
answer: Gradual then sudden degradation points to resource exhaustion or accumulated state. Approach: (1) Check GC logs — most common cause is gradual heap filling. A slow memory leak fills Old Gen, causing increasingly frequent and longer GC pauses. Look for rising Old Gen usage that never drops after GC. (2) Thread pool and connection pool — over time pools exhaust if connections/threads are not returned. Check active vs idle counts at slowdown time. (3) Cache growth without eviction — unbounded HashMap used as cache fills memory. (4) File descriptor or socket exhaustion — unclosed connections accumulate until OS limits hit. (5) Queue buildup — async queue growing faster than consumed. (6) Log rotation or disk full — logging fills disk, causing I/O blocks. Enable GC logging always in production, set up heap trending alerts, capture heap dump before restart.

question: GC pauses are high after a new release. What changes might have caused this?
answer: New release causing high GC pauses means the release changed allocation rate, object lifetimes, or heap structure. Root causes: (1) New cache added — retains large objects, fills Old Gen faster. (2) Increased object allocation rate — new code creating many short-lived objects in tight loops or streams overloads Young Gen, causing frequent Minor GC. (3) Large object allocation — objects above TLAB threshold go directly to Old Gen, bypassing Young Gen, causing premature Full GC. (4) Removed object pooling — previously reused objects now created fresh per request. (5) String concatenation in loops — many intermediate String objects. (6) Thread pool size increase — more concurrent object creation. Diagnosis: compare GC logs before and after release using GCViewer; compare heap dump histograms with jmap -histo to find what newly dominates heap.

question: One API call blocks others even though threads are available. Why?
answer: Available threads but blocking points to a shared bottleneck. Causes: (1) Shared lock — a synchronized block or ReentrantLock serializes all requests through a single critical section regardless of thread availability. (2) Single-threaded downstream — all threads waiting for the same slow response. (3) Database connection pool — all connections in use by one long-running query; threads wait even though they are ready. (4) Rate limiter blocking — threads queue waiting for a permit. (5) @Transactional with long-held DB lock — one transaction holds a row/table lock, blocking all others touching that data. Fix: identify the shared resource using thread dumps (look for identical lock addresses across waiting threads), minimize lock scope, use read-write locks, ensure DB transactions are as short as possible.

question: Your JVM doesn't exit even after main() completes. What's holding it?
answer: JVM stays alive as long as any non-daemon thread is running. Common culprits: (1) ExecutorService with non-daemon threads — Executors.newFixedThreadPool() creates non-daemon threads by default; if shutdown() is never called those threads keep JVM alive. (2) Spring Boot embedded Tomcat — server acceptor thread is non-daemon, intentional for server apps. (3) ScheduledExecutorService threads — non-daemon by default. (4) Timer threads — new Timer() creates a non-daemon thread. (5) Custom threads not marked daemon — thread.setDaemon(true) must be called before start(). (6) Kafka consumer poll loop — infinite poll loop in non-daemon thread. (7) Hanging shutdown hooks. Fix: use daemon threads for background work, always shut down executors explicitly, or call System.exit(0) deliberately.

question: High CPU usage with very low traffic — where do you look?
answer: High CPU with low traffic means CPU work not driven by requests. Investigate: (1) Infinite or tight loops — while(true) or a retry loop without backoff. Take thread dump; offending thread shows RUNNABLE with same stack frame repeatedly. (2) Spin loops — while(!condition){} burns CPU without blocking; should use wait/notify or LockSupport.park. (3) GC thrashing — near-exhausted heap causes GC to run continuously; CPU consumed by GC threads not application threads. Check with jstat -gcutil. (4) Scheduled jobs running too frequently — @Scheduled(fixedDelay=1) doing heavy computation. (5) JIT compilation — transient spike after startup. Use async-profiler or take jstack 3-5 times 1 second apart to find the hot thread.

question: A deadlock happens rarely and can't be reproduced locally. How do you debug it?
answer: Intermittent deadlocks strategy: (1) Capture thread dumps during the hang — configure monitoring to dump threads when response time exceeds threshold. jstack -l includes lock info and JVM annotates 'Found one Java-level deadlock'. (2) Enable programmatic detection — ThreadMXBean.findDeadlockedThreads() returns deadlocked thread IDs; poll this periodically and alert. (3) Analyze lock ordering — deadlocks occur when Thread A holds Lock1 waiting for Lock2, Thread B holds Lock2 waiting for Lock1. Thread dump shows this exactly. (4) Fix with consistent lock ordering — always acquire locks in the same global order (e.g., by object ID). (5) Use tryLock with timeout — if lock not acquired, release held locks and retry. (6) Consider lock-free alternatives — ConcurrentHashMap, AtomicReference.

question: HashMap size increases but lookups become slower. What went wrong?
answer: Poor hashCode quality leads to hash collision clustering. When many keys hash to the same bucket, HashMap degrades from O(1) to O(n) (linked list) or O(log n) (tree in Java 8+ after 8 entries). Root causes: (1) hashCode() always returns same value — all entries in one bucket, HashMap becomes a linked list. (2) hashCode depends only on low-cardinality field — many keys share same bucket. (3) Insufficient initial capacity causing excessive resizing — each resize rehashes all entries. Fix: use Objects.hash(field1, field2, field3) which uses prime multiplication for good distribution. Pre-size the map: new HashMap<>(expectedSize * 4 / 3 + 1). Verify distribution by checking bucket sizes.

question: A background job impacts API response time. How do you isolate it?
answer: Background jobs competing with request-handling threads cause latency spikes due to shared resource contention. Isolation strategies: (1) Separate thread pools — run background jobs on a dedicated ExecutorService separate from the Tomcat request pool. (2) Thread priority — lower thread priority for background jobs: thread.setPriority(Thread.MIN_PRIORITY). (3) I/O isolation — use a separate DataSource/connection pool with lower max size so it cannot starve the request-handling pool. (4) Scheduling windows — run heavy jobs during low-traffic periods using @Scheduled(cron = ...). (5) Rate limiting the job — add Thread.sleep() between iterations or use a Semaphore. (6) Async offloading to Kafka — push work to a message queue consumed by a separate process, fully decoupling it from the API tier.

question: ThreadLocal fixed one bug but created another. What's the risk?
answer: ThreadLocal stores per-thread state but in thread-pool environments threads are reused across requests. Risks: (1) State leakage between requests — if ThreadLocal set for Request A is not cleared before thread returns to pool, Request B on the same thread sees Request A's data (wrong user ID, tenant context — serious data leak). (2) Memory leaks — ThreadLocal values held as long as the thread lives; pooled threads live for application lifetime, so any ThreadLocal not explicitly removed keeps its value alive forever. Especially dangerous with ClassLoader-scoped ThreadLocals on Tomcat redeployment — causes Metaspace leaks. (3) InheritableThreadLocal copies parent values to child threads — can propagate stale context to async tasks. Fix: always call threadLocal.remove() in a try-finally block after the request completes.

question: After scaling instances, performance gets worse. Why?
answer: Counter-intuitive degradation after scaling stems from contention on shared external resources. Root causes: (1) Database connection exhaustion — each instance brings its own HikariCP pool. 10 instances × 20 connections = 200 DB connections, overwhelming PostgreSQL (default max 100). Use PgBouncer for connection pooling. (2) Cache stampede — more instances means more cache-aside reads; on cache miss, multiple instances simultaneously query DB (thundering herd). (3) Distributed lock contention — more instances means more lock contention and wait time. (4) Network overhead — inter-service calls that were local now cross network. (5) JVM warmup — fresh instances start cold, dragging down average performance. (6) Kafka rebalancing — more consumers on same partition leads to rebalancing thrash. Fix: reduce per-instance pool size, implement cache stampede protection, use PgBouncer.

question: A task submitted to ExecutorService fails silently. What happened?
answer: Silent task failure in ExecutorService is a common async bug. When a Runnable submitted via submit() throws an unchecked exception, the exception is caught internally and stored in the Future. If you never call future.get(), the exception is silently discarded. For execute(Runnable), the exception goes to the thread's UncaughtExceptionHandler — if none is set, it prints to stderr which may not be monitored. For submit(Callable), exception is wrapped in ExecutionException and rethrown on future.get(). Fixes: (1) Always call future.get() or add exceptionally(). (2) Set a default UncaughtExceptionHandler on the thread factory. (3) Wrap Runnable in try-catch and log explicitly. (4) Override ThreadPoolExecutor.afterExecute() to intercept all task exceptions.

question: You see frequent Full GCs after a config change. Why?
answer: A config change causing Full GC surge points to heap sizing, object retention, or algorithm changes. Scenarios: (1) Heap size reduced — same workload now fills heap faster. (2) Cache size increased — more objects retained in Old Gen, less room. (3) Connection pool increased — more statement cache entries in heap. (4) Log level set to DEBUG — logging creates many String objects. (5) Thread pool size increased — more concurrent object allocation. (6) MaxMetaspaceSize too low — triggers Full GC to try to unload classes. Class unloading requires Full GC. (7) Off-heap cache disabled — data previously in native memory now allocated on heap. Diagnosis: diff JVM flags before/after, compare GC log metrics using GCEasy.io, run jmap -histo:live before and after to find the class dominating Old Gen.

question: Memory usage keeps growing but no references are obvious. What do you suspect?
answer: Hidden memory growth indicates a non-obvious retention path. Suspects: (1) Static collections — static Map or List that accumulates entries never removed. Static fields are GC roots — everything reachable is never collected. (2) Non-static inner classes — hold implicit reference to enclosing outer class; if stored in a long-lived collection, keeps the outer class alive. (3) Listener/callback registration without deregistration — listener list grows indefinitely. (4) ClassLoader leaks — on app server redeployment, old ClassLoaders referenced by static maps keep all their classes in Metaspace. (5) ThreadLocal without remove() — values accumulate in pooled thread's ThreadLocalMap. (6) Interned Strings on high-cardinality fields — String.intern() on UUIDs fills the string pool. Approach: take heap dumps 1 hour apart, diff with Eclipse MAT's Leak Suspects report.

question: An API is fast sometimes and slow at other times. What could cause this?
answer: Intermittent latency (bimodal distribution) has several causes: (1) GC pauses — Stop-The-World pauses halt all request processing; requests arriving during GC pause experience the full pause as latency. Enable GC logging to correlate pause timestamps with slow requests. (2) JIT compilation — during warmup interpreted code runs slower; cold instances after scaling show this. (3) Connection pool wait — when all DB connections are in use, requests wait up to connectionTimeout (default 30s in HikariCP), causing intermittent spikes. (4) Cache miss — hit is fast, miss triggers DB query; TTL eviction causes miss storms. (5) Downstream service variability. (6) Lock contention — under higher load, contention increases. Monitor with latency percentile tracking — P50 vs P99 divergence reveals intermittent spikes.

question: Application behaves differently between Java 8 and Java 17. Why?
answer: Java version differences cause behavioral changes at multiple levels: (1) Default GC changed — Java 8 uses Parallel GC; Java 9+ uses G1 GC with different pause characteristics. (2) Strong encapsulation (JPMS) — Java 9+ restricts reflective access to JDK internals. Libraries using sun.misc.Unsafe or internal APIs fail with InaccessibleObjectException. Requires --add-opens flags. (3) Compact Strings — Java 9+ uses Latin-1 for ASCII strings — different memory layout. (4) HashMap iteration order may differ across versions — code depending on ordering breaks. (5) Removed APIs — Thread.stop(), SecurityManager, certain sun.* classes removed. (6) Metaspace vs PermGen — Java 8 moved PermGen to Metaspace. Always test with --illegal-access=deny on Java 16 to proactively detect compatibility issues.

question: Logging was increased for debugging and prod went down. How?
answer: Increased logging can bring down production through several mechanisms: (1) Synchronous logging I/O — default Logback appenders write synchronously; every log statement blocks the calling thread until flushed to disk. Under DEBUG at high traffic, request threads spend most time waiting on I/O. (2) Log volume saturating disk — terabytes per hour saturates disk write bandwidth; when OS write buffer fills, log calls block. (3) String formatting overhead — log.debug("Data: " + obj.toString()) always evaluates toString() even if DEBUG is disabled; for complex objects this serialization is expensive. (4) Garbage pressure — millions of log strings create GC pressure. (5) Log aggregation pipeline overwhelmed — Filebeat/Fluentd cannot keep up, filesystem fills. Fix: use parameterized logging, AsyncAppender in Logback, set production level back to INFO/WARN.

question: A fix works locally but fails under concurrency. What was missed?
answer: A fix tested only sequentially may not be atomic. Issues: (1) Assumed single-threaded execution — local testing runs one request at a time; the fix may not be atomic under concurrent access. (2) Shared mutable state — a variable made an instance field (shared) instead of local variable (per-thread). (3) Synchronization on wrong object — synchronized(localVar) or synchronized(new Object()) is meaningless; each thread gets a different lock. (4) Volatile where atomic needed — volatile ensures visibility but not atomicity; volatile int count; count++ is still a race. (5) TOCTOU — condition changed between check and use: if(map.containsKey(k)) { map.get(k) } — another thread can remove the key between check and get; use map.computeIfAbsent() instead. Always write concurrent tests using CountDownLatch.

question: Threads are waiting but no deadlock is detected. What's happening?
answer: Threads waiting without detected deadlock indicates livelock, starvation, or waiting on non-Java-lock resources. (1) Starvation — some threads never get lock access because other threads are more aggressive; blocked threads are in WAITING not BLOCKED state, so JVM deadlock detection (which only checks BLOCKED threads in lock cycles) misses it. (2) Livelock — threads are RUNNABLE and actively retrying but keep yielding to each other — no progress, no blocking. (3) Waiting on external resources — threads blocked on I/O (DB, network, file), not on Java monitors. JVM deadlock detection only detects Java-level monitor deadlocks, not I/O waits. (4) Condition variable — lock.newCondition().await() puts thread in WAITING; if signal is never sent, threads wait forever but it is not a deadlock. (5) Semaphore exhaustion — all permits taken, threads wait on acquire().

question: A cache improves performance initially, then degrades it. Why?
answer: Cache performance degradation over time: (1) Cache memory pressure triggers GC — as the cache grows (no eviction), it fills Old Gen; JVM spends increasingly more time in GC, overwhelming the benefit. (2) Stale data causing retries — expired entries cause business logic to retry or fall back to slower paths. (3) Cache invalidation becoming a bottleneck — with many entries, pattern-based deletion in Redis becomes slow. (4) Hot key contention — a single popular key requested by all threads simultaneously; distributed lock for refresh becomes a bottleneck. (5) Serialization overhead exceeds benefit — heavy deserialization per hit can exceed the DB query cost it avoids. (6) Cache hit ratio degrades — as data set grows but cache size is fixed, hit ratio drops, causing more cache misses than before plus cache overhead. Monitor: hit ratio, eviction rate, GC Old Gen growth.

question: After enabling parallel streams, response time increased. Why?
answer: Parallel streams use the common ForkJoinPool shared across the entire JVM. Enabling parallelism hurts because: (1) Shared ForkJoinPool contention — all parallel stream operations compete for the same common pool (size = CPU cores - 1). Multiple request threads triggering parallel streams simultaneously starve each other. (2) Overhead for small data sets — splitting, task creation, and merging costs exceed speedup for collections under ~10,000 elements. (3) I/O-bound operations — parallel streams help CPU-bound work only; I/O-bound work just adds coordination overhead. (4) Blocking the request thread — ForkJoin workers may be busy, causing the request thread to help execute tasks, preventing it from serving other requests. (5) Thread context lost — MDC and security context are not propagated to ForkJoin threads. Fix: use custom ForkJoinPool or CompletableFuture with dedicated executor.

question: JVM tuning helped latency but hurt throughput. Explain the trade-off.
answer: Latency and throughput are opposing forces in GC tuning. (1) Smaller heap / more frequent GC — each GC cycle pauses shorter; G1 can meet pause time targets (-XX:MaxGCPauseMillis=50). BUT GC runs more frequently, consuming more CPU that could be processing requests — throughput decreases. (2) Larger heap / less frequent GC — GC runs rarely, application threads get more CPU, throughput is higher. BUT when GC runs it pauses longer — P99 latency spikes. (3) Concurrent GC (ZGC/Shenandoah) — does most work concurrently with application, minimizing pauses. BUT concurrent GC consumes CPU even when not actively collecting — lower throughput. The optimal setting depends on SLA: latency-sensitive APIs (payment, trading) prioritize low P99; batch jobs prioritize throughput and tolerate longer pauses.

question: A small code change caused massive GC pressure. How?
answer: Small code changes causing large GC impact typically introduce unexpected high-volume object allocation or prevent garbage collection. Examples: (1) Adding logging in a hot loop — method called millions of times per second creates millions of String objects, filling Young Gen and causing frequent Minor GC. (2) Changing from object reuse to new object creation — e.g., new SimpleDateFormat() per request instead of a shared instance. (3) Removing object pooling — ByteBuffer pool removed, now allocating per request. (4) Returning defensive copy from method called in tight loop — return new ArrayList<>(internalList) on every call. (5) Accidentally holding references — adding object to a static list called per request; list never shrinks. (6) Autoboxing in loops — List<Integer> instead of int[] boxes every element. Profile with async-profiler -e alloc mode.

question: A retry mechanism caused system overload. What design mistake?
answer: Retry storms — retry amplification causing thundering herd — are a critical production anti-pattern. Mistakes: (1) No backoff — retrying immediately means a failing downstream gets hammered with N times the original rate. Downstream, already overloaded, cannot recover. (2) No jitter — even with exponential backoff, if all clients start their retry cycle at the same time they all retry at T+1, T+2 simultaneously — synchronized bursts. Fix: random jitter. (3) No circuit breaker — retries continue indefinitely even when downstream is completely down — cascading failure. (4) Unbounded retry count — holding threads for minutes, filling queue, leading to OOM. (5) Retrying non-idempotent operations — retrying a payment charge that already succeeded causes double charges. (6) Retry amplification — Service A retries 3x to B which retries 3x to C — 1 request becomes 9 requests to C. Always implement: exponential backoff + jitter + circuit breaker + retry budget.

question: App crashes only during peak hours. What patterns do you look for?
answer: Crashes only at peak load indicate resource exhaustion or concurrency bugs amplified by traffic. Investigation patterns: (1) OOM during peak — heap sufficient for normal load but peak traffic creates more concurrent requests, each allocating; aggregate heap usage exceeds max. (2) Thread pool exhaustion + full queue + RejectedExecutionException — during peak, queue fills and rejection handler throws unhandled exception. (3) DB connection pool exhaustion — more requests than available connections; if connectionTimeout expires, exception thrown. (4) File descriptor limit — each HTTP connection or file open consumes an OS file descriptor; peak load hits limit. Check ulimit -n. (5) Cascading timeout — slow downstream makes upstream requests queue; threads fill; new requests rejected. (6) Race conditions only at high concurrency. Profile: heap%, active threads, DB connections, error rate with peak-hour time overlays.

question: What Java decision you made once caused a real production issue?
answer: A common real-world answer: using HashMap in a multi-threaded context without synchronization. During a microservices migration, a shared request-context HashMap was introduced as a class-level field instead of a ThreadLocal to pass metadata between service layers. Under single-threaded load testing it worked. In production under concurrent requests, HashMap's internal state became corrupted — two threads resizing simultaneously caused an infinite loop in the internal linked list (well-known Java 6 issue but still possible with data races). CPU hit 100% and required emergency restart. The diagnostic process took hours. Lesson: (1) Never use non-thread-safe collections in shared scope. (2) Load testing doesn't reproduce concurrency bugs — need dedicated concurrent stress tests. (3) Code review should flag any non-final mutable collection field on a Spring singleton.

What is Spring and Spring Boot? What are the advantages of Spring Boot?
answer: Spring Framework is a comprehensive Java application framework providing dependency injection (IoC), AOP, transaction management, MVC, security, and integration abstractions. It reduced Java EE boilerplate but required significant XML or Java configuration. Spring Boot is an opinionated framework built on top of Spring that makes production-ready applications with minimal configuration. It follows 'convention over configuration'. Key advantages: (1) Auto-configuration — Spring Boot examines the classpath and automatically configures beans. If spring-boot-starter-data-jpa is on the classpath, it auto-configures DataSource, EntityManagerFactory, and TransactionManager. (2) Embedded servers — bundles Tomcat, Jetty, or Undertow. Run as 'java -jar app.jar' without a separate server installation. (3) Starters — curated dependency sets with compatible versions. spring-boot-starter-web pulls Spring MVC, Jackson, Tomcat with aligned versions. No more dependency version conflicts. (4) Actuator — production monitoring endpoints (health, metrics, env) out of the box. (5) Spring Initializr — project scaffolding at start.spring.io. (6) No XML — everything configured in Java or YAML. (7) Production-ready defaults — sensible defaults for connection pools, thread pools, caching.

question: What are Spring Boot starters?
answer: Spring Boot starters are pre-packaged dependency descriptors that bring together all the libraries needed for a particular feature set. Before starters, you had to manually find compatible versions of every library you needed. A single starter dependency transitively brings in all required dependencies with tested, compatible versions. Spring Boot BOM (Bill of Materials) manages all version numbers centrally. Common starters: spring-boot-starter-web (Spring MVC + Tomcat + Jackson), spring-boot-starter-data-jpa (JPA + Hibernate + HikariCP + Spring Data), spring-boot-starter-security (Spring Security), spring-boot-starter-test (JUnit 5 + Mockito + AssertJ + MockMvc), spring-boot-starter-actuator (Actuator endpoints), spring-boot-starter-kafka (Spring Kafka). You can create custom starters for company-wide shared functionality — package your auto-configuration class, list it in META-INF/spring/AutoConfiguration.imports, and publish as a Maven artifact. Teams use custom starters for standardized logging, security config, or observability across all microservices.
codeExample:
<!-- pom.xml — starters manage all transitive dependencies -->
<parent>
    <groupId>org.springframework.boot</groupId>
    <artifactId>spring-boot-starter-parent</artifactId>
    <version>3.2.0</version>
</parent>

<dependencies>
    <!-- Web: Spring MVC + Jackson + Embedded Tomcat -->
    <dependency>
        <groupId>org.springframework.boot</groupId>
        <artifactId>spring-boot-starter-web</artifactId>
    </dependency>
    <!-- JPA: Hibernate + HikariCP + Spring Data JPA -->
    <dependency>
        <groupId>org.springframework.boot</groupId>
        <artifactId>spring-boot-starter-data-jpa</artifactId>
    </dependency>
    <!-- Testing: JUnit5 + Mockito + AssertJ + MockMvc -->
    <dependency>
        <groupId>org.springframework.boot</groupId>
        <artifactId>spring-boot-starter-test</artifactId>
        <scope>test</scope>
    </dependency>
</dependencies>

// Custom starter — company standard logging
// 1. auto-configuration class
@AutoConfiguration
@ConditionalOnMissingBean(LoggingService.class)
public class CompanyLoggingAutoConfig {
    @Bean
    public LoggingService loggingService() {
        return new CompanyLoggingService();
    }
}
// 2. META-INF/spring/org.springframework.boot.autoconfigure.AutoConfiguration.imports
// com.company.starter.CompanyLoggingAutoConfig

question: What is the default port number of a Spring Boot application?
answer: Spring Boot applications run on port 8080 by default when using the embedded Tomcat, Jetty, or Undertow server. This default is defined in the ServerProperties class. To change it: set server.port in application.properties or application.yml. Setting server.port=0 causes Spring Boot to use a random available port — useful for integration tests to avoid port conflicts when multiple tests run in parallel. The actual assigned port can be retrieved from the Environment or by listening to the WebServerInitializedEvent. For management/actuator endpoints, a separate port can be configured with management.server.port. If that is not set, actuator endpoints share the main server port.

question: What is IoC (Inversion of Control)?
answer: Inversion of Control is a design principle where the control of object creation and lifecycle is inverted — instead of objects creating their own dependencies, a framework (the IoC container) creates and manages objects and injects their dependencies. Without IoC, a class creates its own dependencies with 'new', tightly coupling it to specific implementations. With IoC, the class declares what it needs (through constructor parameters, setter methods, or field annotations), and the container provides the implementations. This makes code more modular, testable, and maintainable. In Spring, the IoC container is represented by the ApplicationContext interface. It reads bean definitions (from annotations, XML, or Java config), instantiates objects, resolves dependencies, and manages the full lifecycle. The container is responsible for: (1) Creating bean instances, (2) Wiring dependencies, (3) Calling lifecycle callbacks (@PostConstruct, @PreDestroy), (4) Managing bean scope (singleton, prototype, request, session). The main benefits: loose coupling (programming to interfaces), testability (inject mocks), and centralized lifecycle management.

question: What is Dependency Injection (DI) and what are the types of DI?
answer: Dependency Injection is the mechanism that implements IoC — the container injects required dependencies into objects rather than objects creating them. There are three types: (1) Constructor injection — dependencies provided through the constructor. This is the preferred approach: dependencies are required (cannot create object without them), field is final (immutable after construction), easier to test (just call constructor), and makes circular dependencies immediately visible at startup. (2) Setter injection — dependencies provided through setter methods after object construction. Use for optional dependencies. Can be reconfigured after construction but makes the object mutable. (3) Field injection — dependencies injected directly into fields via @Autowired annotation without constructor or setter. Concise but has significant drawbacks: fields cannot be final, difficult to write unit tests without Spring container, circular dependencies fail silently at runtime, makes mandatory dependencies look optional. Spring recommends constructor injection as best practice. In Spring Boot, if a class has a single constructor, @Autowired is optional — Spring automatically uses it for injection.

question: What is Autowiring? What are the different Autowiring modes?
answer: @Autowired tells Spring to inject a dependency automatically. Spring resolves the bean to inject by type (byType) first — it finds all beans of the matching type in the ApplicationContext. Autowiring modes (relevant in annotation-based Spring): (1) byType (default with @Autowired) — Spring finds a bean matching the declared type. If zero beans match: throws NoSuchBeanDefinitionException (unless required=false). If multiple beans match: throws NoUniqueBeanDefinitionException — resolved with @Qualifier or @Primary. (2) byName — Spring matches the bean name to the field or parameter name. With @Autowired, if multiple beans of the same type exist, Spring falls back to name matching using the field name. (3) @Qualifier — explicitly specify which bean to inject by name when multiple candidates exist. (4) @Primary — mark one bean as the default candidate when multiple exist. (5) constructor — autowiring through constructor (recommended). Legacy XML-based autowiring modes: no (default), byName, byType, constructor, autodetect.

What is a REST API?
answer: REST (Representational State Transfer) is an architectural style for designing networked applications. A REST API uses HTTP as its protocol and treats every piece of data or functionality as a resource identified by a URL. Roy Fielding defined REST in his 2000 dissertation with six constraints: (1) Client-Server — separation of concerns between UI and data storage. (2) Stateless — each request contains all information needed; no session state on the server. (3) Cacheable — responses must define themselves as cacheable or non-cacheable. (4) Uniform Interface — standardized way to interact with resources: resource identification in requests (URL), resource manipulation through representations, self-descriptive messages, HATEOAS. (5) Layered System — client cannot tell if it's connected directly to the end server or an intermediary. (6) Code on Demand (optional) — server can extend client functionality by sending executable code. In practice, REST APIs use HTTP methods (GET, POST, PUT, PATCH, DELETE), return JSON or XML representations of resources, use HTTP status codes for response classification, and are typically versioned in the URL (/api/v1/) or via headers. Most 'REST' APIs in practice are REST-like (RESTful) rather than strictly adhering to all Fielding constraints, particularly HATEOAS.

question: What are the HTTP methods used in REST API?
answer: HTTP defines a set of request methods (verbs) indicating the desired action. In REST APIs: GET — retrieve a resource or collection. Must be safe (no side effects) and idempotent (same result if called multiple times). Responses can be cached. POST — create a new resource. Not idempotent — calling twice creates two resources. Not safe. Returns 201 Created with Location header. PUT — replace an entire resource. Idempotent — calling PUT twice with same data produces same result. If resource doesn't exist, creates it (some implementations). PATCH — partial update of a resource. Not necessarily idempotent depending on implementation (e.g., 'increment by 1' is not idempotent). DELETE — remove a resource. Idempotent — deleting an already-deleted resource returns 404 or 204. HEAD — same as GET but returns only headers, no body. Used to check resource existence or get metadata. OPTIONS — returns allowed methods for a resource. Used in CORS preflight requests. HTTP status code families: 2xx Success (200 OK, 201 Created, 204 No Content), 3xx Redirection (301 Moved Permanently, 304 Not Modified), 4xx Client Error (400 Bad Request, 401 Unauthorized, 403 Forbidden, 404 Not Found, 409 Conflict, 422 Unprocessable Entity), 5xx Server Error (500 Internal Server Error, 503 Service Unavailable).

question: What is JSON and XML?
answer: JSON (JavaScript Object Notation) and XML (eXtensible Markup Language) are two data interchange formats used in REST APIs and data storage. JSON: lightweight, human-readable text format. Supports: objects ({key: value}), arrays ([value1, value2]), strings, numbers, booleans, null. No support for comments, no schema in the format itself (use JSON Schema separately). Native to JavaScript; all modern languages have JSON parsers. Smaller payload than XML (no closing tags). Parsed faster than XML. Default format for Spring Boot REST APIs via Jackson. XML: verbose but expressive markup language. Supports attributes, namespaces, CDATA sections, entity references. Has built-in schema validation (XSD), transformation (XSLT), and querying (XPath/XQuery). Required for SOAP web services. Still common in enterprise, financial, and government systems. More self-documenting but larger payload. In Spring Boot: Jackson (ObjectMapper) handles JSON serialization/deserialization automatically. For XML, Jackson-dataformat-xml or JAXB is used. Content negotiation via Accept header determines which format is returned.

question: What is JWT (JSON Web Token) and OAuth?
answer: JWT (JSON Web Token) is a compact, URL-safe token format for representing claims between parties. A JWT consists of three Base64URL-encoded parts separated by dots: Header.Payload.Signature. Header: algorithm and token type (HS256, RS256). Payload: claims — standard (iss, sub, exp, iat, jti) and custom (userId, roles). Signature: HMAC or RSA signature verifying the token hasn't been tampered with. JWTs are self-contained — the server doesn't need to query a database to validate a token; it verifies the signature and checks expiry. Stateless authentication. OAuth 2.0 is an authorization framework (not authentication) that allows third-party applications to access resources on behalf of a user without exposing credentials. OAuth 2.0 defines four grant types: Authorization Code (web apps — safest), Client Credentials (machine-to-machine), Resource Owner Password (legacy — not recommended), Implicit (deprecated). OpenID Connect (OIDC) is built on top of OAuth 2.0 to add identity (authentication). OAuth gives you an access token (often JWT); OIDC additionally gives an ID token (always JWT) containing user identity. In Spring Security, JWTs are validated using spring-boot-starter-oauth2-resource-server for services that receive JWTs, and spring-boot-starter-oauth2-authorization-server for issuing them.

question: What is Spring Security?
answer: Spring Security is a powerful and highly customizable authentication and access-control framework for Java applications. It integrates with Spring Boot through auto-configuration — simply adding spring-boot-starter-security enables: HTTP Basic authentication, form-based login, CSRF protection, session management, and security headers (X-Frame-Options, X-Content-Type-Options, etc.). Core concepts: (1) Authentication — verifying who you are. SecurityContext holds the Authentication object for the current thread/request. (2) Authorization — what you're allowed to do. Method security (@PreAuthorize, @Secured) and URL-pattern security. (3) Filter chain — Spring Security operates as a chain of servlet filters. Each filter handles one concern: UsernamePasswordAuthenticationFilter (form login), BearerTokenAuthenticationFilter (JWT), ExceptionTranslationFilter (401/403 handling). (4) UserDetailsService — Spring Security interface for loading user information from any source (database, LDAP, in-memory). (5) PasswordEncoder — secure password hashing. BCryptPasswordEncoder is the standard. In Spring Boot 3+, the security configuration uses the SecurityFilterChain bean approach (replacing the deprecated WebSecurityConfigurerAdapter).

question: How do you secure REST APIs in Spring Boot?
answer: Securing REST APIs requires multiple layers. Authentication layer: use JWT tokens issued by an authorization server (Keycloak, Auth0, or Spring Authorization Server). Every API request must include a Bearer token in the Authorization header. The resource server validates the JWT signature, expiry, and audience without a database call. Authorization layer: role-based access control (RBAC) using @PreAuthorize with SpEL expressions or URL-pattern security in SecurityFilterChain. For fine-grained control, use Attribute-Based Access Control (ABAC) where authorization depends on resource attributes. Transport security: always HTTPS. Never expose APIs over plain HTTP in production. Use HSTS header. Input validation: use Bean Validation (@Valid, @NotNull, @Size) on all request bodies and parameters. Validate at controller level, not service level. Rate limiting: prevent abuse with RequestRateLimiter in Spring Cloud Gateway or Bucket4j for per-service limiting. CSRF protection: disable for stateless REST APIs (no session-based CSRF attack is possible). Enable for form-based logins. Security headers: Spring Security adds X-Content-Type-Options, X-Frame-Options, X-XSS-Protection automatically. Add Content-Security-Policy manually.

question: What is Microservices architecture and Monolithic architecture?
answer: Monolithic architecture: the entire application is built as a single deployable unit. All components — UI, business logic, data access — are in one codebase, compiled together, and deployed together as one process. Simple to develop initially, easy to test end-to-end, low operational overhead. Problems at scale: a small change requires redeploying the entire application (risk + slow); scaling means replicating everything (even components that don't need scaling); teams step on each other's code; technology choices are locked; one bug can crash the entire system. Microservices architecture: decompose the application into small, independently deployable services. Each service: owns its bounded context (a specific business capability), owns its data (database per service), is independently deployable, communicates via REST or messaging, can be scaled independently, can use its own technology stack. Benefits: independent deployment, independent scaling, team autonomy, technology diversity, fault isolation. Challenges: distributed systems complexity (network failures, distributed transactions, eventual consistency), operational complexity (many services to deploy, monitor, and trace), service discovery, API versioning. Conway's Law: organizations design systems matching their communication structure. Microservices require cross-functional teams organized around services.

question: How do microservices communicate with each other?
answer: Microservices communicate through two main patterns. Synchronous communication: (1) REST over HTTP — most common. Simple, widely understood. Uses Feign Client (declarative HTTP client) or WebClient (reactive). Drawbacks: tight temporal coupling — if service B is down, service A's request fails. (2) gRPC — protocol buffers + HTTP/2. Binary protocol, faster than JSON/REST, strongly typed contracts. Used for internal service-to-service communication where performance matters. Asynchronous communication: (3) Message brokers (Kafka, RabbitMQ) — services publish events to topics/queues. Consumers process when ready. Temporally decoupled — producer doesn't wait for consumer. Enables event-driven architecture. Higher resilience. Complexity: eventual consistency, message ordering, idempotency. Service mesh (Istio, Linkerd) adds communication features transparently: mTLS, retries, circuit breaking, load balancing without code changes. When to use which: use REST for queries where you need an immediate response; use Kafka/messaging for state-changing commands where you can tolerate eventual consistency; use gRPC for high-throughput internal calls where performance is critical.

question: What is an API Gateway?
answer: An API Gateway is the single entry point for all external requests to a microservices system. It acts as a reverse proxy, routing requests to appropriate backend services. Without it, clients would need to know the address of every service and handle cross-cutting concerns (auth, rate limiting) individually. Core responsibilities: (1) Request routing — map /api/orders/** to order-service, /api/products/** to product-service. (2) Authentication — validate JWT tokens once at the gateway. Downstream services receive enriched headers (X-User-Id, X-Roles) and can trust them without re-validating. (3) Rate limiting — control request rates per client IP or API key using Redis sliding window. (4) Load balancing — distribute traffic across multiple instances of the same service using round-robin or least-connections. (5) SSL termination — handle HTTPS externally, communicate internally over HTTP. (6) Request transformation — add/remove headers, rewrite paths, transform payloads. (7) Circuit breaking — fail fast when a downstream service is unhealthy. (8) Caching — cache GET responses to reduce backend load. (9) Observability — centralized access logging, metrics, distributed trace initiation. In Spring Boot: Spring Cloud Gateway (WebFlux-based, non-blocking) is the recommended solution.
codeExample:
// Spring Cloud Gateway as API Gateway
@SpringBootApplication
public class ApiGatewayApp { ... }

spring:
  cloud:
    gateway:
      default-filters:
        - DedupeResponseHeader=Access-Control-Allow-Credentials,
              Access-Control-Allow-Origin
      globalcors:
        cors-configurations:
          '[/**]':
            allowedOrigins: "*"
            allowedMethods: "*"
            allowedHeaders: "*"
      routes:
        # Order service
        - id: order-service
          uri: lb://ORDER-SERVICE      # Eureka-based LB
          predicates:
            - Path=/api/orders/**
          filters:
            - StripPrefix=1
            - name: CircuitBreaker
              args:
                name: orderCB
                fallbackUri: forward:/fallback
            - name: RequestRateLimiter
              args:
                key-resolver: "#{@userIdKeyResolver}"
                redis-rate-limiter.replenishRate: 100
                redis-rate-limiter.burstCapacity: 200

@Component
public class UserIdKeyResolver implements KeyResolver {
    @Override
    public Mono<String> resolve(ServerWebExchange exchange) {
        // Rate limit per user from JWT
        return Mono.just(
            exchange.getRequest()
                .getHeaders()
                .getFirst("X-User-Id"));
    }
}

question: What is Spring Data JPA?
answer: Spring Data JPA is a layer on top of JPA (Java Persistence API) and Hibernate that dramatically reduces boilerplate data access code. Without Spring Data, you write EntityManager calls for every CRUD operation. Spring Data JPA provides: (1) Repository interfaces — extend JpaRepository<Entity, ID> and get save(), findById(), findAll(), delete(), count() and many more methods for free without writing any code. (2) Query derivation — method name conventions are automatically converted to JPQL: findByEmailAndActive(String email, boolean active) becomes SELECT u FROM User u WHERE u.email = :email AND u.active = :active. (3) @Query — custom JPQL or native SQL for complex queries. (4) Pagination and sorting — Pageable parameter and Page return type for automatic pagination with total count. (5) Specifications — type-safe dynamic queries using JPA Criteria API. (6) Auditing — @CreatedDate, @LastModifiedDate automatically populated using @EnableJpaAuditing. (7) Projections — interfaces or DTOs for selecting specific columns. (8) Spring Data REST — automatically exposes repositories as REST endpoints (use carefully in production). The key insight: Spring Data generates the implementation of your repository interface at runtime using byte manipulation.

How do you handle exceptions in Spring Boot?
answer: Spring Boot provides several levels of exception handling. (1) @ExceptionHandler on a single controller — handles exceptions thrown only by that controller. Limited scope. (2) @RestControllerAdvice (or @ControllerAdvice + @ResponseBody) — global exception handler. Catches exceptions from all controllers. The recommended approach for REST APIs. Map specific exceptions to appropriate HTTP status codes with meaningful error bodies. (3) ResponseStatusException — throw directly from controllers/services with an HTTP status code without needing a separate handler method. (4) ProblemDetail (Spring Boot 3+ / RFC 7807) — standardized error response format with type, title, status, detail, instance fields. Enable with spring.mvc.problemdetails.enabled=true. (5) ErrorController — for errors that occur in filters (before reaching controllers), implement ErrorController to handle /error mapping. Key principles: never expose internal exception messages (stack traces) to clients; always return structured error responses; log the full exception server-side; use appropriate HTTP status codes (400 for validation, 401 for auth, 404 for not found, 409 for conflict, 500 for unexpected).

question: What is application.properties or application.yml?
answer: application.properties and application.yml are the primary externalized configuration files in Spring Boot. They store configuration values that the application reads at startup. Spring Boot reads them from multiple locations in precedence order (higher overrides lower): classpath:/, classpath:/config/, file:./, file:./config/. application.properties uses key=value format; application.yml uses YAML's hierarchical structure which is more readable for nested properties. Both are loaded automatically by Spring Boot's PropertySourcesLoader — no explicit annotation needed. Key uses: server configuration (port, SSL), datasource configuration (URL, credentials), Spring profiles activation, logging levels, feature flags, any custom application properties. Profile-specific files (application-dev.yml, application-prod.yml) activate additional properties for specific profiles, overriding base file values. In Spring Boot 2.4+, config files can import other files with spring.config.import=optional:file:/etc/secrets.properties allowing secrets to be mounted from external sources. Environment variables automatically override any property using relaxed binding: MY_DB_PASSWORD overrides spring.datasource.password.
codeExample:
# application.properties format
server.port=8080
server.servlet.context-path=/api

spring.datasource.url=jdbc:postgresql://localhost:5432/mydb
spring.datasource.username=dbuser
spring.datasource.password=${DB_PASSWORD} # env var interpolation
spring.datasource.hikari.maximum-pool-size=20

spring.jpa.show-sql=false
spring.jpa.open-in-view=false

logging.level.com.example=INFO
logging.level.org.hibernate.SQL=DEBUG

# Custom properties
app.max-upload-size-mb=10
app.jwt.secret=${JWT_SECRET}
app.jwt.expiry-seconds=3600

---

  port: 8080

    context-path: /api

spring:

    url: jdbc:postgresql://localhost:5432/mydb

Your HashMap suddenly starts performing very slowly with large data. What internal behavior could cause this?
answer: HashMap performance degrades with large data due to hash collision clustering or excessive resizing. When many keys hash to the same bucket, the bucket becomes a long chain (linked list in Java 7, red-black tree in Java 8+ after 8 entries). A chain of N entries degrades lookup from O(1) to O(N) or O(log N). Causes: (1) Poor hashCode() — returns same value for all objects, or has very low entropy. (2) Load factor threshold — default 0.75 means a 16-capacity map resizes at 12 entries. If initial capacity too small for large data, frequent resizing occurs. Each resize rehashes all entries. (3) High collisions from data patterns. Fix: provide adequate initial capacity at construction (new HashMap<>(expectedSize / 0.75 + 1)), ensure quality hashCode(), consider LinkedHashMap for insertion-order or TreeMap for sorted keys.

question: A ConcurrentModificationException appears randomly in production. What coding mistake might cause it?
answer: ConcurrentModificationException (CME) is thrown when a collection is structurally modified while being iterated with a fail-fast iterator. The iterator checks an internal modCount on each next() call and throws CME if it changed. Common mistakes: (1) Removing from a List while iterating with for-each — remove() increments modCount, next iteration throws CME. (2) Multi-threaded access — one thread iterates while another adds/removes — classic race with non-thread-safe ArrayList. (3) Calling a method inside the loop that modifies the same collection. Fixes: (1) Use Iterator.remove() which is safe. (2) Use removeIf() — list.removeIf(item -> condition) — atomic. (3) CopyOnWriteArrayList for infrequent writes. (4) Collect items to remove separately, then call removeAll().

How do you secure REST APIs in Spring Boot?
answer: Securing REST APIs requires multiple layers. Authentication layer: use JWT tokens issued by an authorization server (Keycloak, Auth0, or Spring Authorization Server). Every API request must include a Bearer token in the Authorization header. The resource server validates the JWT signature, expiry, and audience without a database call. Authorization layer: role-based access control (RBAC) using @PreAuthorize with SpEL expressions or URL-pattern security in SecurityFilterChain. For fine-grained control, use Attribute-Based Access Control (ABAC) where authorization depends on resource attributes. Transport security: always HTTPS. Never expose APIs over plain HTTP in production. Use HSTS header. Input validation: use Bean Validation (@Valid, @NotNull, @Size) on all request bodies and parameters. Validate at controller level, not service level. Rate limiting: prevent abuse with RequestRateLimiter in Spring Cloud Gateway or Bucket4j for per-service limiting. CSRF protection: disable for stateless REST APIs (no session-based CSRF attack is possible). Enable for form-based logins. Security headers: Spring Security adds X-Content-Type-Options, X-Frame-Options, X-XSS-Protection automatically. Add Content-Security-Policy manually.

What is the exact startup flow of a Spring Boot application?
answer: Spring Boot startup is a precise sequence of phases orchestrated by SpringApplication.run(). (1) SpringApplication object creation — sets up default settings, detects WebApplicationType (SERVLET, REACTIVE, or NONE based on classpath), loads SpringApplicationRunListeners from META-INF/spring.factories. (2) Environment preparation — creates ConfigurableEnvironment, processes all property sources (command-line args, system properties, env vars, application.yml/properties), fires EnvironmentPreparedEvent, activates profiles. (3) Banner printing — reads the banner from banner.txt or custom BannerProvider. (4) ApplicationContext creation — creates AnnotationConfigServletWebServerApplicationContext (for web apps). (5) Context preparation — registers shutdown hook, applies ApplicationContextInitializers, fires ApplicationContextInitializedEvent. (6) Context load — registers main class bean definition, loads all @Configuration classes, fires ApplicationPreparedEvent. (7) Context refresh (the main phase) — triggers AbstractApplicationContext.refresh() which: loads bean definitions (component scan, @Configuration processing via ConfigurationClassPostProcessor), instantiates all singleton beans (including embedded server creation), fires ContextRefreshedEvent. (8) ApplicationRunner and CommandLineRunner execution — runs in order. (9) ApplicationReadyEvent — application is fully started and ready to serve requests.

question: Difference between @ComponentScan and @SpringBootApplication?
answer: @SpringBootApplication is a meta-annotation that combines three annotations: @SpringBootConfiguration (which is itself @Configuration), @EnableAutoConfiguration, and @ComponentScan(basePackages). So @SpringBootApplication includes @ComponentScan implicitly. The difference: @ComponentScan alone only triggers classpath scanning. It does NOT enable auto-configuration. You would use @ComponentScan separately to add additional scan packages to an existing application or to explicitly control the scan configuration when you don't want the opinionated defaults. @SpringBootApplication = @Configuration + @ComponentScan + auto-configuration. The basePackages of @ComponentScan within @SpringBootApplication defaults to the package of the annotated class. If your main class is in com.example, Spring scans com.example and all sub-packages. Common mistake: placing the main class in the default package (no package declaration) causes Spring to scan the entire classpath — extremely slow and may pick up beans from library JARs. Always place the main class in a named package at the root of your domain.

question: How does Spring Boot detect embedded Tomcat automatically?
answer: Spring Boot detects embedded Tomcat through its auto-configuration mechanism backed by conditional annotations. When spring-boot-starter-web is on the classpath, it brings in spring-boot-starter-tomcat which includes the Tomcat JAR. The auto-configuration class ServletWebServerFactoryAutoConfiguration is registered in META-INF/spring/AutoConfiguration.imports. Inside this class, @ConditionalOnClass({Servlet.class, Tomcat.class}) checks that both classes are present on the classpath. If they are, TomcatServletWebServerFactory bean is created. This factory is then used by the context refresh phase: when EmbeddedWebApplicationContext refreshes, it calls onRefresh() which calls createWebServer() which uses the factory to create and start the embedded server. The server starts before any @PostConstruct methods run. If you exclude Tomcat and add Jetty or Undertow, the corresponding @ConditionalOnClass triggers that factory instead — only one server starter should be on the classpath. If you add server.port=0, Tomcat binds to a random port. The WebServerInitializedEvent is fired once the server is bound.

question: What happens if two beans of the same type exist without @Qualifier?
answer: When Spring finds multiple candidates for a single injection point (same type, no qualifier), it follows a resolution algorithm: (1) It first checks for @Primary — if one bean is marked @Primary, it wins. (2) It then tries name matching — if the field/parameter name matches a bean name, it uses that bean. (3) If neither applies, Spring throws NoUniqueBeanDefinitionException at startup. This is a configuration error, not a runtime error, so the application will not start. Exception message explicitly lists all matching bean names. In Spring Boot, this is especially common with auto-configuration — a library creates a default bean, and you define your own. The fix is: mark your custom bean with @Primary (it overrides the library default), or use @Qualifier at the injection point, or exclude the auto-configuration class that creates the conflicting bean. Testing note: in @SpringBootTest, all beans are loaded — duplicates that only exist in one environment can surface in tests.

question: How does Spring Boot load profile-specific configurations?
answer: Spring Boot's profile system activates environment-specific configuration files. When a profile is active (e.g., 'prod'), Spring loads: application.yml (base, always loaded), then application-prod.yml (profile-specific, overrides base). Profile-specific values take precedence over base values for the same key; keys only in the base file are still available. Profile activation sources (in precedence order): spring.profiles.active in application.properties (lowest), SPRING_PROFILES_ACTIVE environment variable, --spring.profiles.active=prod command-line argument (highest). Multiple profiles can be active simultaneously: spring.profiles.active=cloud,prod — both application-cloud.yml and application-prod.yml are loaded, with prod taking precedence over cloud if both define the same key. In Spring Boot 2.4+, multi-document YAML uses spring.config.activate.on-profile: instead of spring.profiles. Spring Cloud Config adds another layer: profile-specific files are served from the config repo: {application}-{profile}.yml. Programmatic profile check: @Profile("prod") on a @Bean or @Configuration activates that bean only in the prod profile.

question: What is the role of SpringFactoriesLoader?
answer: SpringFactoriesLoader is the mechanism Spring Framework uses to discover and load extension points declared in META-INF/spring.factories files found on the classpath. This is the backbone of Spring Boot's auto-configuration and SPI (Service Provider Interface) pattern. How it works: (1) At startup, Spring Boot calls SpringFactoriesLoader.loadFactoryNames() with a given type (e.g., EnableAutoConfiguration). (2) It scans all JARs on the classpath for META-INF/spring.factories files. (3) Each factory file maps interface/annotation names to implementation class names. (4) All matching class names are returned and instantiated. In Spring Boot 2.7+, auto-configurations moved to a separate file: META-INF/spring/org.springframework.boot.autoconfigure.AutoConfiguration.imports (one class name per line), making it faster to load than parsing a properties file. SpringFactoriesLoader is used for: auto-configurations, ApplicationContextInitializers, ApplicationListeners, SpringApplicationRunListeners, FailureAnalyzers. When building custom starters, you register your auto-configuration class in this file so Spring Boot discovers it automatically.

question: How did Spring Boot remove XML configuration almost completely?
answer: Spring Boot's elimination of XML is built on two foundational features: Java-based configuration and conditional bean registration. Java-based configuration (@Configuration + @Bean) was introduced in Spring 3.0 — a @Configuration class is a factory for bean definitions using pure Java, with IDE support, type safety, and refactoring capability that XML lacks. Spring Boot builds on this with conditional configuration: @ConditionalOnClass, @ConditionalOnMissingBean, @ConditionalOnProperty allow beans to be configured only when appropriate. This means library authors write auto-configurations in Java that activate automatically based on classpath presence. Component scanning (@ComponentScan, @Service, @Repository) eliminates manual bean registration. Spring profiles (@Profile) replace environment-specific XML files. Property injection (@Value, @ConfigurationProperties) replaces XML property-placeholder. AOP (@Aspect, @Around) replaces XML AOP configuration. JPA (@Entity, @Id, @OneToMany) replaces persistence.xml. Spring Security (@EnableWebSecurity, @Bean SecurityFilterChain) replaces security XML namespaces. The only remaining use of XML in Spring Boot is when integrating with legacy systems or frameworks (like CXF for SOAP, some Batch configurations) that don't yet have Java config equivalents.

question: Difference between @RestController and @Controller internally?
answer: @RestController is a composed annotation that is literally @Controller + @ResponseBody at the annotation level. Spring processes them identically in most ways — both are registered as beans via component scanning, both are detected by RequestMappingHandlerMapping to build the URL routing table. The internal difference is in how the return value of handler methods is processed. When a method in a @Controller returns a String, Spring tries to resolve it as a view name via ViewResolver (Thymeleaf, FreeMarker, JSP). When a method is annotated with @ResponseBody (either directly or via @RestController), Spring invokes HttpMessageConverter chain to serialize the return value directly to the response body. Jackson's MappingJackson2HttpMessageConverter handles Java objects to JSON. The converter is selected based on the response Content-Type and the Accept header from the client. ModelAndView return type is only relevant for @Controller (not @RestController). ResponseEntity works with both and takes precedence — it carries the HTTP status and headers explicitly. Internally, both are proxied by Spring AOP for @Transactional and other AOP concerns.

question: How does Spring Boot manage dependency versions automatically?
answer: Spring Boot manages dependency versions through its parent POM (spring-boot-starter-parent) and the Bill of Materials (BOM) spring-boot-dependencies. The parent POM inherits from spring-boot-dependencies which declares <dependencyManagement> with hundreds of tested, compatible library versions. When you declare a dependency in your pom.xml without a <version> tag, Maven uses the version declared in spring-boot-dependencies. This ensures all libraries work together. The parent POM also configures the build: Java version, resource filtering, plugin versions (spring-boot-maven-plugin). If you cannot use the parent POM (your project has its own parent), import the BOM instead. Gradle: use platform() to import the BOM. Override a managed version by declaring it in your pom.xml <properties> section (e.g., <jackson.version>2.16.0</jackson.version>). Spring Boot's tested combinations are validated with its own integration test suite — a major benefit is that random library upgrades are less likely to break things compared to managing versions manually.
codeExample:
<!-- Option 1: Parent POM (most common) -->
<parent>
    <groupId>org.springframework.boot</groupId>
    <artifactId>spring-boot-starter-parent</artifactId>
    <version>3.2.0</version>
</parent>

<!-- No version needed — managed by parent -->
<dependencies>
    <dependency>
        <groupId>org.springframework.boot</groupId>
        <artifactId>spring-boot-starter-web</artifactId>
    </dependency>
    <dependency>
        <groupId>com.fasterxml.jackson.core</groupId>
        <artifactId>jackson-databind</artifactId>
        <!-- version managed by parent -->
    </dependency>
</dependencies>

<!-- Override a managed version -->
<properties>
    <jackson.version>2.16.1</jackson.version>
</properties>

<!-- Option 2: BOM import (when you have your own parent) -->
<dependencyManagement>
    <dependencies>
        <dependency>
            <groupId>org.springframework.boot</groupId>
            <artifactId>spring-boot-dependencies</artifactId>
            <version>3.2.0</version>
            <type>pom</type>
            <scope>import</scope>
        </dependency>
    </dependencies>
</dependencyManagement>

// Gradle equivalent
plugins {
    id 'org.springframework.boot' version '3.2.0'
    id 'io.spring.dependency-management' version '1.1.4'
}
dependencies {
    implementation 'org.springframework.boot:spring-boot-starter-web'
    // No version — managed by dependency-management plugin
}

question: What is the complete lifecycle of a Spring Bean?
answer: The Spring Bean lifecycle is a precise sequence: (1) Bean definition loading — Spring reads @Component, @Configuration, XML to create BeanDefinition objects (metadata, not instances). (2) BeanDefinitionRegistryPostProcessor — modifies bean definitions before factory post-processing. (3) BeanFactoryPostProcessor — modifies bean definitions (e.g., PropertySourcesPlaceholderConfigurer resolves @Value placeholders). (4) Instantiation — Spring calls the constructor (or factory method) to create the instance. (5) Populate properties — setter injection and @Autowired field injection. (6) Aware interfaces — setBeanName() (BeanNameAware), setBeanFactory() (BeanFactoryAware), setApplicationContext() (ApplicationContextAware). (7) BeanPostProcessor.postProcessBeforeInitialization() — processes @PostConstruct, applies AOP advice, initializes @Validated, etc. (8) InitializingBean.afterPropertiesSet(). (9) Custom init-method (from @Bean(initMethod=...) or XML). (10) BeanPostProcessor.postProcessAfterInitialization() — finalizes AOP proxy creation. Bean is now fully initialized and in the context. On shutdown: (11) @PreDestroy method. (12) DisposableBean.destroy(). (13) Custom destroy-method. Note: prototype scoped beans are NOT destroyed by the container — the caller is responsible.

question: How does Spring Boot handle externalized configuration?
answer: Externalized configuration is a core Spring Boot feature — the same application code should work in different environments (dev, test, prod) with different configuration values. Spring Boot loads configuration from 17 property sources in strict precedence order (first listed wins): (1) devtools global settings, (2) @TestPropertySource, (3) @SpringBootTest properties, (4) command-line arguments, (5) SPRING_APPLICATION_JSON, (6) ServletConfig init parameters, (7) JNDI attributes, (8) System properties (System.getProperties()), (9) OS environment variables, (10) RandomValuePropertySource, (11) Profile-specific application properties outside jar, (12) Application properties outside jar, (13) Profile-specific application properties inside jar, (14) Application properties inside jar, (15) @PropertySource, (16) Default properties. In practice: config files provide defaults; environment variables override for runtime (secrets, URLs); command-line args for quick overrides during testing. Spring's relaxed binding maps environment variables to property keys: MY_APP_DB_URL maps to my.app.db.url or myApp.dbUrl. Spring Cloud Config adds Config Server as an additional source with the highest application-level precedence.
